summary: A place in the sun A golden sunrise blooms over the Homer Spit last week across a clear sky that later clouded up and dropped a blanket of snow. The National Weather Service forecast for the Kenai Peninsula today calls for sunny skies, a high of 25 and a low of 15. title: Board to review Inlet silver rules
summary: Board to review Inlet silver rules By JON LITTLE Daily News Peninsula Bureau SOLDOTNA - Valentine's Day weekend in Girdwood sounds like a romantic rendezvous for snowboard lovers, but it's also the time and place where commercial fishermen, anglers and biologists will hunker down, once again, to talk about fish. The state Board of Fisheries exhaustively reviewed Cook Inlet fisheries less than a year ago in Soldotna and recently agreed with Gov. Tony Knowles that it needs to take a harder look at how the region's silver salmon are managed. The meeting is scheduled for Feb. 11-13. State fisheries policy for the Inlet is usually reviewed every three years, but poor runs in some Inlet streams led Knowles to call for a special meeting. Word that the board will take up silver salmon management w…

title: BP will rethink its plans
summary: BP will rethink its plans By BEN SPIESS Daily News reporter In the proposed deal with BP Amoco, the state would get the oil fields and exploration acres officials believe they need to draw a new oil company to the North Slope. But for BP, the agreement opens a series of new concerns. After the announcement Friday, BP Exploration (Alaska) Inc. president Richard Campbell said the company will reassess jobs, investment plans and oil production. Since BP announced the Arco takeover April 1, the company broadcast a string of predictions: * $5 billion investment on the North Slope over five years - a 25 percent increase over old spending plans. * An oil production increase of 150,000 barrels a day by 2004 - a 15 percent increase. * A layoff of 400 Arco employees - a number later to reduced to 37…

title: Peninsula wood-chip producer defaults on bank loans
summary: Peninsula wood-chip producer defaults on bank loans By JON LITTLE Daily News Peninsula Bureau SOLDOTNA - Machinery that converts the Kenai Peninsula's beetle-infested spruce trees into exportable wood chips has ground to a halt, at least temporarily. Circle DE Pacific, which has shredded trees from the Peninsula's ailing forests and sold the chips to Japanese paper manufacturers since 1992, has defaulted on $5.4 million in loans. Low prices fueled by Japan's recession and a glut of wood chips on the market are to blame, company officials say. But Circle DE's failure to make loan payments has led its creditor, National Bank of Alaska, to sell off the company's loading facility on the Homer Spit to make its money back. The $5 million facility's centerpiece is a 4-acre concrete pad near the H…

title: Halibut hoax nets journalists
summary: Halibut hoax nets journalists BY KAREN AHO Daily News reporter It seemed plausible enough - at least in the landlocked plains of eastern Nebraska. A golf-bag tag from Nancy Hoshor's local course, with her name scribbled on it, turns up inside the belly of a halibut hooked in Southeast Alaska. She'd never golfed in the 49th state, or anywhere on the West Coast. But she had vacationed in Hawaii, 10 years ago. It could happen, right? A Hawaiian Pacific halibut (genus Nonexistent) swallows the tag and swims 2,600 miles to the north Pacific, where a fisherman hooks it a decade later and extracts the tag, address intact. That's the story that an Alaska fishing lodge manager told in a note to the Fremont Golf Club in Fremont, Neb. A golf pro opened it and, recognizing a good tale, called not only…

title: Eagle River teen wins $2.75 million NHL pact
summary: Eagle River teen wins $2.75 million NHL pact By DOYLE WOODY Daily News assistant sports editor Eagle River's Ty Jones, the strapping teenager who once said he would buy a car when he signed his first professional hockey contract, can start cruising auto dealerships now. And he doesn't have to limit his shopping to economy models. Jones, 19, on Thursday night said he has signed a three-year contract worth $2.75 million, including a guaranteed signing bonus of nearly $1.4 million, with the Chicago Blackhawks of the National Hockey League. "It's a pretty big deal, I guess," Jones said as he chuckled over the telephone while paging through a copy of his contract in his Chicago hotel room. "It's a great deal. I can't complain." Jones, a 6-foot-3, 215-pound power forward with a polite, humorous …

title: Microsoft foe hired to review BP deal
summary: Microsoft foe hired to review BP deal By ROBERT KOWALSKI Daily News Juneau Bureau JUNEAU - The U.S. Justice Department's lead lawyer on the Microsoft Corp. antitrust case, David Boies, will be representing Alaska in its review of BP Amoco's proposed $26 billion purchase of Atlantic Richfield Co. Attorney General Bruce Botelho hired Boies last week. Botelho said Boies will be part of a team of experts the state is assembling to study the acquisition that would join Alaska's two largest oil producing companies. "We're looking to make sure that we have the best and brightest this nation has to offer in advancing the state's interest in analyzing this," Botelho said. "Obviously, one of the major considerations is antitrust," Botelho said. "I doubt that anyone would question Mr. Boies' credenti…

title: Copyright © 1999 The Anchorage Daily News
summary: Hi, is Regis home? Game show's fans phone Alaska By SHEILA TOOMEY Daily News reporter Contestants must pass a series of tests to get on ABC's exorbitantly successful quiz show, "Who Wants to Be a Millionaire." The first test seems to be dialing the show's phone number correctly. Instead of the machine that answers calls to the New York show, hundreds of would-be millionaires have found themselves chatting with employees at the Atlantic Federal Credit Union in downtown Anchorage. "We get them from morning to night," said Alexandra Kalugin. Most callers are friendly, if confused. "One guy told me he was good at Jeopardy and that's why he was calling," Kalugin said. "He said he just got divorced .... He was in Idaho." "Millionaire" is running every night during television's November sweeps, a…

title: Court limits advocates in child custody cases
summary: Court limits advocates in child custody cases The Associated Press JUNEAU - The state Supreme Court has issued rules limiting the use of children's advocates in private custody disputes such as divorces. The rules could save parents money and make it easier for them to challenge allegations against them, but some judges believe the rules mean there will be less information available when they decide who gets custody of children. "I think there's a potential loss to the kids," said Juneau Superior Court Judge Larry Weeks. "It's hard to see how they gain." The rules, which take effect April 15, set qualifications and roles for advocates and custody investigators. They also make it easier for lawyers to cross-examine guardians who make allegations against parents. "In our system, the right of…

title: Troopers work to close book on fabled crash
summary: Troopers work to close book on fabled crash By PETER PORCO Daily News reporter The 30 men were in their late teens to mid-50s. Most were in the merchant marine. Many had also served in the armed forces. One took part in the Allied invasion of Normandy on June 6, 1944. And one of the 30, Alaska State Troopers learned Wednesday, wore a ring on the night of March 12, 1948, when their chartered Northwest Airlines DC-4 rammed into Mount Sanford at 230 mph. The silver ring has the image of a mosque etched on its diamond-shaped face, and "1946" and "Iran" stamped on its sides. Troopers said they found the ring under rocks and imbedded in the ice of an unnamed glacier on Sanford's west side, in Wrangell-St. Elias National Park. Troopers also brought back a perfectly preserved, still frozen forearm…

title: Ships legally dump in Passage
summary: Ships legally dump in Passage By PAULA DOBBYN Daily News reporter Cruise ships plying the Inside Passage routinely dump polluted water in spots known as "doughnut holes," areas just beyond three miles offshore. The dumping is legal because the foreign-flagged vessels are not regulated under the federal pollution law known as the Clean Water Act, according to government officials. No one knows exactly how much pollution is being dumped, because it's not monitored. But judging by the size of the holding tanks aboard the colossal ships, a senior official with the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency in Juneau said, the amount of waste is far beyond what Anchorage pumps out daily into Cook Inlet. "These are floating cities," EPA official Steve Torok said. But unlike factories and municipalitie…

title: Story of missing bike in Palmer has oodles of small-town charm
summary: Story of missing bike in Palmer has oodles of small-town charm By Mike Doogan Laura Beckley is 39, and she doesn't have a driver's license. She has a learner's permit now and she's taking driving lessons, but when she wants to go somewhere, she rides her bicycle. "My bike is like my life," she said. "Everybody is used to seeing me on my bike." Beckley lives in Palmer, so when she says everybody, she pretty much means everybody. The Chamber of Commerce says 4,141 people live in Palmer, but it feels smaller. Particularly the old downtown, the collection of business buildings and homes strung along the railroad tracks. Beckley lives downtown, and she works the counter at the coffeehouse there, Vagabond Blues. So there's a fair share of Palmer that knows her by sight, if not by name. One of th…

title: Copyright © 1999 The Anchorage Daily News
summary: Police probe hazing Allegation cites sexual contact By MATT NEVALA Daily News reporter The Anchorage Police Department is investigating a hazing incident that resulted in the suspension of 12 Service High School football players last week amid allegations that sexual contact also occured. Service principal Steve Cline on Monday confirmed the investigation. "The Anchorage Police Department asked for a list of students we interviewed in connection with the hazing and a copy of any statements we took from the students," Cline said. "They believe there may have been a sexual contact while we were on that trip." Cline and Anchorage School District officials have found no evidence of any sexual behavior, schools superintendent Bob Christal said. Police confirmed that they are looking into the ha…

title: Copyright © 1999 The Anchorage Daily News
summary: Laughing all the way Stand-up skills keep rail-riding tourists rolling By ELIZABETH MANNING Daily News reporter NORTH OF HURRICANE GULCH - Following the instructions of a guide, the tourists stuck thumbs to their temples, spread out fingers to look like moose antlers and bent and wiggled them. "This is the moose wave," explained Joan Cullinane, who works for Princess Tours. "It's fun because you can end up in someone's photo album." As a guide on the train between Anchorage and Fairbanks, Cullinane seats people for meals and assists the bartender. Above all, she talks about Alaska and keeps passengers entertained - for 12 hours at a stretch. That's when gimmicks like the moose wave come in handy. On a recent northbound trip, the time for the wave came as two Alaska Railroad trains passed e…

title: Environmentalists offer judge steps to protect Stellar sea lions
summary: Environmentalists offer judge steps to protect Stellar sea lions By NATALIE PHILLIPS Daily News reporter Having won the first round in a lawsuit against the government to further protect the endangered Steller sea lion, an environmental coalition has proposed to a federal judgewhat should happen next, including making the federal government write new pollock-fishing regulations and redoing the environmental impact statement the judge found inadequate. The coalition asked District Court Judge Thomas Zilly to order the new regulations be put in place by Nov. 1, in time for the January pollock fishery in the Bering Sea. And it asked to have a new environmental statement done by Oct. 1, 2000, according to the motion filed Monday. "It's a big job. That's why we are saying they should have until…

title: Proposed ordinance locks in Sullivan weekend dates for Aces
summary: Proposed ordinance locks in Sullivan weekend dates for Aces By ELIZABETH MANNING Daily News reporter Anchorage Aces hockey games and other existing sporting events would get top priority when it comes to booking dates at the Sullivan Arena under a measure introduced Tuesday before the Anchorage Assembly. The goal behind the ordinance is to make sure the Anchorage Aces don't lose any more weekend dates. Over the last two years, the Aces have lost several weekend dates to new events, said Aces general manager Derek Donald. "We've gone backwards," Donald said. "We're not looking to change the current schedule. We're just hoping not to lose any more weekends." Over the summer, the professional hockey team had said it might sue the city over the loss of weekend home dates. But instead of taking…

title: Railroad cars derail; crews fight fuel leak
summary: Railroad cars derail; crews fight fuel leak By LARRY CAMPBELL Daily News reporter Ten Alaska Railroad tank cars, each filled with 20,000 of jet fuel, derailed early Sunday morning north of Talkeetna, and one leaking tank may have spilled more than 7,000 gallons of the fuel. Railroad crews and state and federal environmental observers were working through late Sunday evening to pump the fuel from all the tanks, including the damaged one. Absorbent materials were spread and artificial dams were built to keep the fuel in trackside drainage ditches and away from the surrounding marsh area. "It appears it's pretty well contained right around the spill area now," said Brad Hahn, state on-scene coordinator for the Department of Environmental Conservation. "It's fortunate, considering the mess we'…

title: C-O-M-P-E-T-I-T-I-O-N!
summary: C-O-M-P-E-T-I-T-I-O-N! Cheerleading squads from around Alaska gathered at Bartlett High School on Saturday for the 12th State Cheerleading Competition. During rehearsals Saturday afternoon, Houston High School cheerleader Alesha Shell stretched in the hallway. (MARC LESTER / Anchorage Daily News) Back | Top | Home | User Agreement Problems? title: Renegade guide remains at large
summary: Renegade guide remains at large By LIZ RUSKIN Daily News reporter Fugitive hunting guide Michael "Tony" Roberts has been on the lam for six weeks, and authorities say the trail has gone cold. Roberts, 37, ran away Sept. 16 from his hunting camp west of Talkeetna as a trooper helicopter was closing in at the end of an undercover sting. His last known whereabouts were at Big Lake on Sept. 20. "It's pretty much just anybody's guess," said Lt. Franco D'Angelo, of the state Division of Fish and Wildlife Protection. "He could have left the state. He could still be up in that area." Authorities say the Anchorage resident baited grizzly bears, sold bear gall bladders and killed an undersized bull moose - all of which is illegal. He told undercover officers posing as hunting clients they could shoo…

title: Feds take fisheries control
summary: Feds take fisheries control Knowles says he'll try again for amendment By DON HUNTER, LIZ RUSKIN and DAVID WHITNEY Daily News reporters Alaskans awoke Friday to find the federal government in control of a big part of the state's vast fisheries resources for the first time since statehood. The takeover of subsistence fishing regulation on waters flowing through federal lands took place at the stroke of midnight, after the state Senate failed to agree on a constitutional amendment that would have allowed voters to recognize a rural priority for subsistence use of fish and game. The priority is guaranteed in the 1980 Alaska National Interest Lands Conservation Act. State officials, lawmakers and fishing groups say the federal managers sooner or later will begin regulating at least some commer…

title: Agencies study ships' pollution
summary: Agencies study ships' pollution Companies say they dump responsibly By PAULA DOBBYN Daily News reporter A major review of state and federal pollution laws is under way to toughen the rules governing the booming cruise ship industry in Alaska. Cruise ships are not required to have air or water pollution permits or oil spill contingency plans, unlike other industries and cities that must meet strict environmental standards. "It's appropriate that these types of vessels come under the same types of limits that are imposed on communities," said Steve Torok, a senior official at the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency in Juneau. Critics say cruise lines are getting off practically scot-free. "The mining, logging and many other industries have to jump through major environmental hoops. These fo…

title: Budget plan taps into fund
summary: Budget plan taps into fund 'Endowment' may avert taxes By ROBERT KOWALSKI Daily News Juneau Bureau JUNEAU - The House Republican majority agreed Thursday to a plan that would combine Permanent Fund earnings and the state's main reserve account to create an "endowment" to balance the state budget. The approach, which the Republicans agreed to in a closed-door afternoon session, does not include new taxes, according to lawmakers present. Its goal is to pay Alaskans dividends of about $1,000 annually for the first three years and then increase, they said. That would be less than the Permanent Fund dividends in each of the past three years; last year's was $1,541. But House Speaker Brian Porter said final details of the proposal, including the potential need for taxes and the size of dividends…

title: Senate panel nixes budget amendments
summary: Senate panel nixes budget amendments By PAUL QUEARY The Associated Press Alaska State Legislature See related pages: http://www.legis.state.ak.us/ JUNEAU - Republicans on the Senate Finance Committee rejected dozens of budget amendments Monday, but put off action on the highest-profile question before them - whether to restore some of the nearly $48 million in aid to local communities slashed by the House. Most of the attempts to change the operating budget for the fiscal year that begins July 1 were offered by Sen. Al Adams of Kotzebue, the lone committee Democrat. The vote was usually 1-8, with the eight Republicans on the panel in opposition. Among other proposals, the lopsided majority defeated attempts to send more money to the Department of Corrections to house prisoners out of state…

title: Machines in Denali spur suit
summary: Machines in Denali spur suit Snowmobile critics attack park policy By PETER PORCO Daily News reporter The other shoe dropped on the National Park Service on Wednesday when conservationist groups sued the agency because it had allowed snowmachines in some areas of Denali National Park. Back in February, snowmachiners sued the agency in federal court for the opposite reason - because its temporary regulation allowed them to ride in two small zones but nowhere else in the 2 million acre heart of Denali National Park. Snowmachines were already permitted in the 4 million acres of park and preserve lands that were added to the old Mount McKinley park by the 1980 Alaska National Interest Lands Conservation Act. Snowmachiners claimed the right to also ride in the original2 million acres, designate…

title: Arco christens first of three new tankers
summary: Arco christens first of three new tankers By ALAN CLENDENNING The Associated Press NEW ORLEANS - A massive double-hulled tanker built for Arco Marine Inc. and designed to comply with post-Exxon Valdez oil spill laws was christened Saturday at Avondale Industries' shipyard near New Orleans. The 125,000-ton Arco Endeavor is the first of three "millennium-class" tankers being built by Avondale to carry North Slope oil between Valdez and the Arco refinery near Bellingham, Wash. The $140 million vessels are the first crude-oil tankers designed to meet standards mandated in the Oil Pollution Act of 1990, which phases out single-hulled tankers. Congress acted after the 1989 Exxon Valdez tanker grounding, which dumped nearly 11 million gallons of North Slope crude into Prince William Sound. The Ex…

title: Copyright © 1999 The Anchorage Daily News
summary: Gallows humour Brits' impending takeover of Arco inspires wry jokes By BEN SPIESS Daily News reporter Arco Alaska president Kevin Meyers gave a presentation last month at an oil industry meeting, delivering the normal rap: the BP takeover, how it's good for Alaska and that despite the job losses, there is opportunity ahead. In fact, he told the crowd, slipping his resume into the slide show, if anybody is looking for an executive experienced in running a multinational oil firm, he is ready to talk. The room broke up. Meyers, who often plays amateur comedian in front of crowds, will, with several hundred co-workers, lose his job if BP takes over Arco in the proposed $30 billion buyout. What with layoffs and talk of Alaska's becoming the latest British colony, with the state's oil riches in …

title: State, federal agencies garnishee PFDs
summary: State, federal agencies garnishee PFDs By MAUREEN CLARK The Associated Press Alaskans in bankruptcy or arrears on college loans, child support, taxes and other payments could see little or none of this year's $1,769.84 Permanent Fund dividend. State, federal and local agencies are first in line to collect tens of millions of dollars in fines, taxes, loans and other delinquent payments as the state distributes checks from its oil wealth savings account. Final figures on the number of dividends being garnisheed this year are not yet available. Last year, bill collectors went after the dividends of more than 44,000 Alaskans who owed a total of $38.7 million - an average of $877.38 each. First in line to collect is U.S. Bankruptcy Court, followed by the state Child Support Enforcement Division…

title: Juneau puts tax on tourists
summary: Juneau puts tax on tourists Cruise-ship travelers to pay $5 By PAUL QUEARY The Associated Press JUNEAU - Cruise-ship tourists will pay $5 each just to visit Alaska's capital after voters approved a head-tax initiative fueled by explosive growth in tourism and a cruise line's admission of toxic dumping. The city expects an annual take of about $3 million from the estimated 600,000 passengers who visit each year - if the fee survives possible legal challenges. Although a similar measure proposing a $7 fee failed three years ago, this year's version passed with nearly 70 percent of the vote. Greg O'Claray, one of the initiative's sponsors, credits the industry's recent growth for part of the shift. "There's excesses of too much traffic, 51 helicopters buzzing the air daylight to dusk, all of …

title: Kids get friends in court
summary: Kids get friends in court Volunteers represent interests of children By MOLLY BROWN Daily News reporter Since she doesn't have a lot of money to give, she gives her time. With a slight Southern accent and a warm smile, Denise Henderson said her love for kids brought her to the Office of Public Advocacy's CASA program. Henderson first heard of CASA, the Court Appointed Special Advocate program, when she worked in the juvenile division of the Albuquerque District Attorney's Office. It was a job in which she saw kids who had substance-abuse problems and were victims of child abuse and broken homes. "I'm a single parent," Henderson said. "It opened my eyes to what was going on." After living for 32 years in New Mexico, Henderson spent four years in Gulfport, Miss. For almost two years now, she…

title: School enrollment drop surprises officials
summary: School enrollment drop surprises officials Teachers face transfers to even out load By ROSEMARY SHINOHARA Daily News reporter Enrollment in Anchorage public schools has fallen 1,150 below predictions, leaving district officials scrambling to reassign teachers where they're needed most. The drop was a surprise. Just a few weeks ago, the district expected enrollment to increase by 650 for a total of 50,200. The district had been growing steadily since 1989. The new projection is for 49,000 students by the end of September, 550 fewer than the number enrolled at the same time last year. Assistant superintendent Carol Comeau said the district will not need to lay off teachers. It had held off hiring its last round of teachers, waiting to see how many children arrived at school. Classes started …

title: Seed center taps tough plants
summary: Seed center taps tough plants Mat-Su facility hopes to harvest traits special to Alaska species By S.J. KOMARNITSKY Daily News Mat-Su Bureau BUTTE - Plants in Alaska have to be hardy to survive harsh winters, long summer days, and often poor soils. Now that tenacity has earned them a new home, one that will preserve them for future generations and potentially benefit everyone from farmers to medical patients. The new home is the Arctic Germplasm Repository. It's a kind of souped-up seed bank where arctic plants are being collected, stored and studied for what they may promise in higher crop yields, new drugs and other benefits. Germplasm is leaves, seeds, flowers or any other part of a plant that includes its genetic code. The federally funded center is the first of its kind in Alaska and …

title: Copyright © 1999 The Anchorage Daily News
summary: Safer ships urged Panel targets small vessels By PAUL QUEARY The Associated Press JUNEAU - A Coast Guard-led task force is calling for more accurate charts and more attentiveness aboard small cruise ships after at least four recent accidents. Unlike larger cruise ships, smaller vessels ply remote areas of Alaska's Inside Passage, where sheer rock walls and active glaciers are only a few yards away. About 50 vessels measuring between 50 feet and 200 feet carry 200,000 passengers a year in Alaska, said Capt. Ed Page, chief of the Coast Guard's marine safety division for Alaska and chairman of the Alaska Small Vessel Task Force. "There's been a proliferation of these operations, smaller passenger vessels going into more remote areas of Alaska, going closer to the hazards to show people a good…

title: Fairbanks drug raid stalls taxis for several hours
summary: Fairbanks drug raid stalls taxis for several hours The Associated Press FAIRBANKS - Police shut down two Fairbanks taxi companies to conduct a drug raid that netted several ounces of marijuana and a small amount of heroin. Cars stopped running for Eagle Cab and Yellow Cab for five hours Thursday while officers from the Statewide Drug Enforcement Unit searched for drugs, Alaska State Troopers said. The marijuana was discovered in an operations building, and investigators discovered the heroin in a rented building on the property, which is owned by Alaska Transportation Unlimited, the operator of the two taxi services, Sgt. J.R. Roberts said. "We've been looking into an allegation of drug sales coming out of there for a couple of months now," Roberts said. Cora P. Chappell, 48, was arrested …

title: Natives dangle wetlands easement as trade for forest road
summary: Natives dangle wetlands easement as trade for forest road By DAVID WHITNEY Daily News Washington Bureau WASHINGTON - Native-owned Chugach Alaska Corp. signaled Wednesday that it might consider selling a conservation easement to its timberlands and other acreage near Cordova, but not before the U.S. Forest Service grants it a road easement to the property through the Chugach National Forest. A conservation easement, strongly advocated by environmentalists and the Clinton administration, would give the struggling Native regional corporation perhaps tens of millions of dollars for investment in exchange for an agreement not to log its property in and around the Copper River Delta. Chugach owns about 73,000 areas near Carbon Mountain. If a conservation easement to the land were sold to the fed…

title: City aids seniors' home
summary: City aids seniors' home Problem-plagued facility in Chugiak gets $65,000 boost By LISA DEMER Daily News reporter The Anchorage Assembly has approved a $65,000 bailout for the financially troubled Chugiak Senior Center. Assembly members Dan Kendall and Anna Fairclough, who represent Eagle River-Chugiak, said Friday that the money isn't for any specific program. Rather, it is intended to see the center through the next few months while it sorts out finances. Bill Lester, a board member serving as the unpaid interim executive director, approached Kendall for the emergency appropriation, approved by the Assembly last week. "The choice was to let them run out of money in a couple of months or to give them a boost to get back on their feet," Kendall said. The center includes independent-living a…
